findings.title: Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains.

findings.title: Missing Content Security Policy
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources.

findings.title: Missing X-Content-Type-Options
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: x-content-type-options was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses.

findings.title: Public admin path is reachable
findings.description: One or more common CMS or generic admin paths responded successfully to safe passive checks.
findings.evidence: /wp-login.php returned 200
findings.remediationHint: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible.

findings.title: CMS fingerprint was detected
findings.description: Public page evidence revealed a likely CMS fingerprint. This is informational unless combined with other risk evidence.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; evidence generator:WordPress 6.4, marker:wordpress
findings.remediationHint: Keep CMS core, extensions, and themes patched, and remove unnecessary public version disclosures where practical.

findings.title: WordPress 6.4 requires patch review
findings.description: The scanner observed a WordPress 6.4 generator string. This MVP treats the version as a known-risk demo match when confidence is high, without claiming exploitation.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210
findings.remediationHint: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.

findings.evidence: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources.

findings.title: Missing X-Content-Type-Options
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: x-content-type-options was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses.

findings.title: Public admin path is reachable
findings.description: One or more common CMS or generic admin paths responded successfully to safe passive checks.
findings.evidence: /wp-login.php returned 200
findings.remediationHint: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible.

findings.title: CMS fingerprint was detected
findings.description: Public page evidence revealed a likely CMS fingerprint. This is informational unless combined with other risk evidence.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; evidence generator:WordPress 6.4, marker:wordpress
findings.remediationHint: Keep CMS core, extensions, and themes patched, and remove unnecessary public version disclosures where practical.

findings.title: WordPress 6.4 requires patch review
findings.description: The scanner observed a WordPress 6.4 generator string. This MVP treats the version as a known-risk demo match when confidence is high, without claiming exploitation.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210
findings.remediationHint: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.

findings.title: Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains.

findings.evidence: x-content-type-options was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses.

findings.title: Public admin path is reachable
findings.description: One or more common CMS or generic admin paths responded successfully to safe passive checks.
findings.evidence: /wp-login.php returned 200
findings.remediationHint: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible.

findings.title: CMS fingerprint was detected
findings.description: Public page evidence revealed a likely CMS fingerprint. This is informational unless combined with other risk evidence.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; evidence generator:WordPress 6.4, marker:wordpress
findings.remediationHint: Keep CMS core, extensions, and themes patched, and remove unnecessary public version disclosures where practical.

findings.title: WordPress 6.4 requires patch review
findings.description: The scanner observed a WordPress 6.4 generator string. This MVP treats the version as a known-risk demo match when confidence is high, without claiming exploitation.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210
findings.remediationHint: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.

findings.title: Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains.

findings.title: Missing Content Security Policy
findings.description: A baseline browser security response header was not observed in the passive HTTP response.
findings.evidence: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.
findings.remediationHint: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources.

report.title: Technical Remediation Report for Coyoacan
report.summary: Coyoacan is currently assessed at critical risk with score 83/100 based on 6 normalized reportable finding(s). This technical report preserves uncertainty, distinguishes evidence from inference, and orders remediation work by severity, evidence clarity, and verification readiness.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 2: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Cuauhtemoc
friendly.summary: Cuauhtemoc shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

report.title: Technical Remediation Report for Chihuahua
report.summary: Chihuahua is currently assessed at critical risk with score 83/100 based on 6 normalized reportable finding(s). This technical report preserves uncertainty, distinguishes evidence from inference, and orders remediation work by severity, evidence clarity, and verification readiness.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 2: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Saltillo
friendly.summary: Saltillo shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Torreon
friendly.summary: Torreon shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Durango
friendly.summary: Durango shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

technical.title: Technical Remediation Report for Chimalhuacan
technical.summary: Chimalhuacan is currently assessed at critical risk with score 83/100 based on 6 normalized reportable finding(s). This technical report preserves uncertainty, distinguishes evidence from inference, and orders remediation work by severity, evidence clarity, and verification readiness.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 2: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Zapopan
friendly.summary: Zapopan shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 2: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

findings.title: WordPress 6.4 requires patch review
findings.description: The scanner observed a WordPress 6.4 generator string. This MVP treats the version as a known-risk demo match when confidence is high, without claiming exploitation.
findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.
findings.remediationHint: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.
findings.evidenceSummary: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

report.title: Technical Remediation Report for Reynosa
report.summary: Reynosa is currently assessed at critical risk with score 83/100 based on 6 normalized reportable finding(s). This technical report preserves uncertainty, distinguishes evidence from inference, and orders remediation work by severity, evidence clarity, and verification readiness.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 2: Enable HSTS after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

friendly.title: Friendly Remediation Report for Veracruz
friendly.summary: Veracruz shows a critical level of risk with score 83/100 based on 6 main issue(s). This version explains the situation in simpler language, keeps the uncertainty visible, and highlights the most important next actions first.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Why it matters: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.

Priority 2: Enable forced secure browsing rule after confirming HTTPS is stable for the site and subdomains. Why it matters: strict-transport-security was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 3: Add a tested Content-Security-Policy that limits script, frame, and object sources. Why it matters: content-security-policy was not present in the selected response headers.

Priority 4: Send browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Why it matters: browser setting that blocks unsafe file-type guessing was not present in the selected response headers.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Why it matters: /wp-login.php returned 200.

priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

findings.evidence: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.
findings.remediationHint: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.
findings.evidenceSummary: wordpress 6.4; confidence 0.8; references CVE-2024-31210.
remediationSteps: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release.
Assign an owner and due date before treating the item as resolved.
verificationSteps: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Record the post-change result for wordpress 6.4 requires patch review and compare it with the current evidence.

convexPersistenceArgs.summary: Zapopan is currently assessed at critical risk with score 83/100 based on 6 normalized reportable finding(s). This technical report preserves uncertainty, distinguishes evidence from inference, and orders remediation work by severity, evidence clarity, and verification readiness.
priorityActions: Priority 1: Confirm the WordPress core and plugin patch level, then upgrade to the latest supported release. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.

Priority 4: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on HTML and static asset responses. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
Priority 5: Restrict administrative entry points with access controls, MFA, and monitoring where operationally possible. Verification: Re-run the same bounded check after the mitigation is applied.
